text:
Recent years have witnessed explosive developments of ma-
chine learning (ML)  in various industry sectors like au-
tonomous driving, content generation, and healthcare. As the
ML models become increasingly large in size (e.g., the GPT-3
language model has 175 billion parameters [14]), obtaining
high-quality models that satisfy business needs requires mas-
sive amount of training data, which makes data a valuable
resource to fuel the AI industry. Under this background, data
markets naturally emerge as an intermediate platform, for
1

Affordable Blockchain Data Market for
gh Off-Chain Processing
gzhe Liu  Mengqi Chen
UST(GZ)  HKUST(GZ)
model owners to purchase intended data to facilitate their
model training, and for data owners to trade the value of their
private data for profit [31].
Conventional data markets are often implemented in a
centralized fashion as a cloud application, on which data
providers and consumers register themselves as users. To
facilitate effective and satisfactory data trading, a suite of
mechanisms are designed and implemented on the market to
realize desirable functionalities like data pricing and match-
ing between providers and consumers [18, 20, 26, 36]. While
simple to implement, centralized designs are faced with se-
vere security threats, such that the entire service is disrupted
when several cloud servers or a datacenter is compromised.
This security concern spurs the recent paradigm shift for data
market design towards decentralized architectures, especially
blockchains [10, 21, 25, 42, 43]. A blockchain system (e.g.,
Bitcoin [38] and Ethereum [54]) consists of a large number of
decentralized nodes, who agree on and execute sequences of
transactions through consensus algorithms and cryptographic
tools, even when a subset of nodes are corrupted by adver-
sary. Decentralized data market requires substantially more
resources for attackers to take over, hence significantly en-
hancing the security of the market.
Blockchain-based data markets leverage smart contracts [1]
to securely implement the trading rules. A naive implementa-
tion would have the data providers and the consumers include
their data and requests respectively in blockchain transactions,
which will be uploaded and processed by blockchain nodes
(or verifiers). This is faced with two major challenges for
building an ML data market: 1) efficiency and affordability:
the size of the training data or ML model parameters is quite
large, which would incurs a prohibitively high computational
cost for the verifiers and monetary cost for data trading par-
ticipants; 2) privacy: as the contents of the transactions are
transparent to public, private data and proprietary model pa-
rameters may be leaked. To address the first challenge, many
existing solutions choose to store the data onto a remote file
system (e.g., IPFS [13]), and record the commitment of the
data on-chain (see, e.g., [21, 42, 43]). However, this approach

2.1  Federated learning on blockchains
Federared learning (FL) [35] is an emerging collaborative
learning paradigm, in which a central server, collects from
distributed clients local models trained over their private data,
and aggregates them into a global model. Recent works
have proposed to implement FL over blockchains, espe-
cially to counter security threats from malicious server (see,
e.g., [24, 29, 33, 34, 39, 47, 56]). Blockchain-based FL frame-
works can be generally categorized into two types, depending
on whether modifications are needed for the underlying con-
sensus layer of blockchain, or FL is implemented as a smart
contract application. For the first type, for instance, a commit-
tee formation and leader selection mechanism for blockchain
consensus is proposed based on the qualities of the locally
trained models [33]; in [29, 47], a committee of nodes verify
the validity of the local models, and submit the aggregation of
valid models to include in a block. In contrast, smart contract-
based FL systems [30, 44, 46, 48, 57] implement all the model
verification and aggregation operations as codes in smart con-
tracts, leaving the underlying consensus layer untouched. We
adopt the smart contract-based approach to design our data
market, such that it can be flexibly utilized with any layer-1
solutions that support smart contracts.
2.2  Scaling techniques for smart contracts
Rollups, as the most promising technique to scale the execu-
tion of smart contracts, move the verification of transactions
off-chain, significantly reducing the execution time and gas
cost on layer-1 blockchain [50]. There are mainly two types of
rollups: optimistic rollups and zero-knowledge (ZK) rollups.
In optimistic rollups, off-chain nodes execute transactions and
upload transactions and new state roots to the blockchain, with
a fraud-proving scheme allowing blockchain clients to chal-
lenge rollup results [23]. In ZK rollups, an off-chain operator
performs batch processing of the transactions, and generates
validity proofs (by e.g., zk-SNARKs [12]) to be uploaded on
chain. These proofs provide evidence that the state transitions
are indeed carried out executing the transactions. For both
optimistic and ZK rollups the transaction data are replicated
on blockchain miners.
Developed upon ZK rollups, validiums further move the
storage of the transactions off layer-1 blockchain, further
improving scalability and reducing gas cost. Major validium
systems, such as StarkEx [7], ensure off-chain data availability
by establishing a data availability committee (DAC), which
consists of a trusted group of entities responsible for storing
state copies and providing evidence of data availability. While
the adoption of DAC simplifies the system implementation,
the security of the system relies on trust in DAC members.
There is a potential risk for the transaction data to become

3.1  Data market for training ML models
We consider the problem of data trading over a data mar-
ket, for machine learning applications (e.g., prediction and
classification). There are two types of players on the market:
• Model owner (MO): the users who would like to obtain
an ML model for certain tasks, but has only a limited
amount of local data. MO intends to purchase more train-
ing data through the data market to improve the model
performance;
• Data owner (DO): the users who are willing to contribute
the utility of their private data for model training, and in
return obtain payoffs from the MO.
Consider  N  DOs  who  contribute  to  the  model
training.  Each  DO  n  has  locally  a  dataset  Sn =

the gradient computed from DOs’ data, and η is the learning

One way to implement this training process is for a central-
ized party (e.g., a cloud server) to collect all the data from the
DOs and carry out training. However, various privacy, secu-
rity, and trust issues may arise. Firstly, uploading private data
directly causes the DOs to surrender the exclusive ownership
of their data, which is highly undesirable; Second, selfish or
malicious DOs can provide garbage or poisoned data, to obtain
undeserved rewards or harm the model performance; Finally,
the centralized party becomes a single vulnerability of the
system, such that once compromised, no data privacy, model
performance, and trading legitimacy can be guaranteed. This
motivates us to design a data market that achieves accurate
and efficient training of ML models, with minimum privacy
leakage and maximum trustworthy guarantees.
3.2  Security goals
We explicitly list the desired privacy and security require-
ments as follows:
• Avoiding single point of failure. The functionalities of a
data market should be realized by a decentralized imple-
mentation, for which corrupting a part of system resource
will not reveal private data and compromise the correctness
of the data trading logic.
• Model privacy (for MO). An MO would like to keep its
model private from all other parties, before and after the
training process.
• Data privacy (for DO). A DO would like to maintain the
ownership of its local data, and keep them private from all
other parties.
• Security against malicious DOs. The data market should
verify the legitimacy of the information provided by the DOs,
protecting the model from being poisoned by ill-formed
results from malicious DOs.
• Security against malicious MO. The data market should en-
force that honest DOs who faithfully follow the protocol get
properly compensated for their contributions to the model
training.

4.1  Cryptographic primitives
Arithmetic circuit. An arithmetic circuit C over a finite field
Fq of prime order q is a computational graph whose vertices
consist of input, addition, and multiplication gates. The input
gates hold the values of the inputs to the circuit C, and the
output wire of an addition or multiplication gate carries the
addition or multiplication of the input wires for that gate.
Shamir secret sharing. A (T,K) threshold Shamir secret
Fm
sharing is a scheme that creates K shares of a secret sss ∈  q of
length m, such that any T shares reveal no information about
sss; and any T +1 shares can be used to perfectly reconstruct sss.
The basic scheme consists of the following algorithms.

Secret-shared non-interactive proof (SNIP). A SNIP proto-
col [15] allows a client to prove to a group of servers, that its
Fm
private input sss ∈  q is valid, i.e., Valid(sss) = 1 for some pred-
icate Valid(·), which is represented as an arithmetic circuit
C. The protocol would like to keep the proof zero-knowledge,
such that for an honest client, any proper subset of curious
servers know nothing about sss other than that Valid(sss) = 1;
and for a malicious client whose input is invalid, as long as all
servers are honest, they will reject the input with overwhelm-
ing probability.

4.2  Federated learning with model perturba-
tion
Federated learning (FL) is an emerging collaborative learn-
ing paradigm, where a group of clients (analogous to the DOs
in our setting) each with some local private data, collabo-
rate to train an ML model with the coordination of a central
server. With the objective of minimizing the function F(WWW)

4.3  Blockchain and smart contract
Introduced by Satoshi Nakamoto [38], blockchain is a spe-
cialized form of distributed ledger maintained by Internet-
connected nodes. A blockchain consists of a sequence of.

5.1  System overview
The proposed OmniLytics+ data market consists of an on-
chain smart contract ModelTrade and a cluster of K off-chain
servers. As shown in Figure 1, the overall data trading func-
tionality is implemented by ModelTrade, which executes
on the verifiers of the underlying blockchain network. To
speed up the contract execution and reduce the transaction
fees, OmniLytics+ further augments ModelTrade with off-
chain servers to offload storage and computation burden from
blockchain. Specifically, using the encrypted model of MO
whose commitment is posted on chain, each DO computes its
local gradient, and secret shares it with the off-chain servers.
The servers collectively verify the validity of the gradient
from the shares, such that the final (light-weight) validation
result is reconstructed on chain. This also triggers the pay-
ments to the validated DOs for their contributions to model
training. Next, secret shares of all validated gradients are ag-
gregated at each server to form a single share of aggregation,
and the commitment to the aggregated gradient is computed
on chain. Finally, the aggregated gradient is reconstructed and
decrypted by the MO.

Figure 1: System overview of the proposed OmniLytics+
data market. OmniLytics+ consists of an on-chain smart con-
tract ModelTrade and an off-chain server cluster. The inter-
actions between different parties in a data trading session are
illustrated and numbered by their orders of occurrence.
To verify the validity of a DO’s gradient without leaking
its local data, OmniLytics+ employs the SNIP protocol be-
tween the DO and the servers, with the validation result posted
on chain. To ensure the integrity of the computation results
against malicious servers, OmniLytics+ utilizes Shamir se-
cret sharing for the SNIP protocol, and adopts robust recon-
struction (i.e., Reed-Solomon decoding) for validity check.
5.2  Validation predicate
To prevent selfish or malicious DOs from uploading garbage
or faulty results, OmniLytics+ performs validity check for
each DO’s result ggg. Specifically, we employ a validation pred-
icate Valid(·) that outputs 1 when ggg is tested to be valid,
and 0 otherwise. We consider a class of validation predi-
cates that can be represented as an arithmetic circuit over a
finite field Fq. For instance, for the norm threshold test [49],
Valid(ggg) = 1[||ggg|| ≤ ρ] and only inputs with norms less
2
than the threshold ρ are considered valid. The parameters
(e.g., ρ in the norm threshold test) for the validity test are
provided by the MO, which are computed from its local data.
5.3  ModelTrade workflow
A data trading session on OmniLytics+ is accomplished
through an invocation of the smart contract ModelTrade,
which implements a single iteration of the model update as
in (3), with the initial model from the MO, and the gradients
collected over participating DOs. The model perturbation tech-
niques described in Section 4.2 are employed to protect the
model privacy of the MO. Each DO performs the SNIP protocol
with off-chain servers to verify the validity of its gradient in
a privacy-preserving manner. While the original SNIP does
not guarantee soundness in presence of malicious servers,
we modify it with Shamir secret sharing and utilize robust

reconstruction (i.e., Reed-Solomon decoding) to ensure cor-
rectness of verification results when (up to a certain number
of) off-chain servers are untrusted.

Figure 2: State transition of the smart contract ModelTrade.
The eight states of ModelTrade are represented by ovals.
State transitions are triggered by either applying a method (in
a solid box), or occurrence of an event (in a dashed box).
To start, an MO deploys ModelTrade with training reward
deposit on the blockchain. As shown in Figure 2, ModelTrade
transitions  between eight  states,  i.e., Setup, Register,
ShareCollection,  ShareReady,  GradValidation,
Payment, Reconstruction, and Finished.
Step 1: Model publication. Upon deployment, ModelTrade
is in the Setup state, with a set of DOs the MO would like to pur-
chase data from specified by a whitelist() method.

• Required number of data points for each participating DO to
compute its gradient, denoted by M0;
• Maximum number of distinct DOs to collect gradients from,
denoted by N.
Executing start() moves ModelTrade into the Register
state, and the contract starts to register for the DOs who intend
to participate in data trading. The registration is done when N
DOs have registered, or a timeout is triggered.

Minimizing commitment size. As the dimension m of QQQn
is often large due to the large number of parameters in
an ML model, utilizing conventional verifiable secret shar-
ing schemes like Feldman’s scheme [17] could incur high
computational and monetary costs on the contract. Here we
adopt a vector commitment scheme from [22] to construct
a constant-size (does not scale with m) commitment CCCn to
QQQ . Specifically, for a cyclic group G with a generator g,

If the above check does not pass, server i issues a complaint,
and DO n sends the share [QQQn]i  to ModelTrade for further
verification.
The ModelTrade contract maintains a set V of valid DOs.
All DOs who have its shares verified by all servers (or the
contract in the case where malicious servers file fraudulent
complaints on correct shares) are added to V by the end of
this step. It is also important to note that thanks to verifiable
secret sharing, it is guaranteed that each honest server has a
correct share of QQQn, for all n ∈ V .
Step 3: Private gradient validation. At the beginning of
this step, the ModelTrade contract reveals the arithmetic cir-
cuit for evaluating the Valid(·) predicate provided by the
MO. We design this to be done after the DOs’ encrypted gradi-
ents are shared with off-chain servers, to eliminate the pos-
sibility for a malicious DO to meticulously craft its data to
escape the validity check (e.g., performing projected gradient
descent to bypass norm-based defenses for injecting model
backdoors [52]).
For the announced Valid(·) circuit, each DO n executes
the SNIP protocol with the K servers, using Shamir secret
shares instead of additive shares in the original proposal [15].
Specifically, DO n evaluates Valid(QQQn), and obtains a poly-
nomial h(x) of degree at most 2(M−1) as described in Sec-
tion 4.1, where M is the number of multiplication gates in.

6.1  Security analysis
We analyse the proposed OmniLytics+ data market to illus-
trate how it satisfies the security goals listed in Section 3.2.
8

Avoiding single point of failure. Building OmniLytics+ on
top of a decentralized blockchain system and realizing the data
trading logic as a smart contract effectively alleviate the secu-
rity risk due to single point of failure. Harassing the security
guarantees of the underlying blockchain system, the market
remains to operate correctly even if a subset of blockchain
nodes are compromised. For instance, an implementation of
OmniLytics+ on top of Ethereum would execute the trading
logic correctly, even if up to 50% of the blockchain nodes are
controlled by adversaries.
Model privacy. The privacy of the model parameters is pro-
tected by the model perturbation technique described in Sec-
tion 4.2. Only the encrypted model parameters are revealed
on the contract, and all the subsequent operations performed
on the DOs, the off-chain servers, and the contract are over the
ciphertext. As shown in [55, Theorem 4], the probability for
any party in the OmniLytics+ system to obtain true values of
model parameters, other than the MO who has the private key
L
((RRRℓ)ℓ= , γγγ), equals to 0.

(0)
Cn  relies on the Discrete Logarithm (DL) assumption, in
the sense that any efficient algorithm in breaking the hiding
property of the commitment can be used to solve the DL prob-
lem efficiently (see proof of Theorem 1 in [22]). In addition,
QQQn is also secret shared among, and jointly verified by the
off-chain servers. The privacy of QQQn is protected by the se-
curity guarantees of Shamir secret sharing and the adopted
SNIP protocol. Specifically, QQQn is perfectly secure against no
more than T colluding servers, for all n ∈ [N]. Finally, the

Security against malicious DOs. The validity of the submit-
ted result from each DO is verified by the Valid(·) predicate.
The soundness property of the adopted SNIP protocol guar-
antees that any malicious DO who does not submit a valid
gradient (according to Valid(·)) will be rejected with over-
whelming probability, in the case where all servers are honest.

inal SNIP protocol [15] does not promise soundness against
malicious servers, the proposed OmniLytics+ system enables
robustness against malicious servers, via modifying SNIP
with Shamir verifiable secret sharing and Reed-Solomon de-
coding. Specifically, consider up to A servers are malicious
and can arbitrarily deviate from the prescribed protocol. By

7.1  Implementation
We implement our proposed OmniLytics+ as an Ethereum
smart contract EthModelTrade, with 400 lines of Solidity
code [41]. We deploy the contract on our local Geth Ethereum
Testnet [4] by Remix IDE [6] and ganache [2]. We implement
the DOs and off-chain servers using Go and C++ (for FFT-
based polynomial operations, built on the NTL library [3]),
enabling multiple DOs and servers to operate on a local ma-
chine. We quantize the gradient values onto the finite field
F 127  . For our implementation of ZK rollups, we utilize
2  −1
the zokrates library [9] to produce Groth’16 zero-knowledge
proofs, preserving the transaction data. The rollup operator
aggregates these transactions, and creates a ZK-SNARK proof
for validation, and then submits this proof alongside a con-
densed version of the transactions to the Ethereum blockchain.
We implement the model training on the MO and the DOs using
Python and Pytorch [5]. We utilize Web3py library [8] to
connect the off-chain parts and the smart contracts.
Gradient validation circuit. We adopt the norm threshold
test as the validation method for gradients, i.e., Valid(ggg) =
1[||ggg||2 ≤ ρ], for some threshold ρ provided by the MO. We
implement the validation check of the form ||ggg||2 ≤ ρ as an
arithmetic circuit computing (||ggg||2 −1)×. . .×(||ggg||2 −ρ).
Specifically, for validation a gradient of size m, our circuit
(cid:0) m  2  (cid:1)  m  2  m  2
outputs ∑i=1 gi −1 ×(∑i=1 gi −2)×. . .×(∑i=1 gi −ρ). If
the value equals zero, this indicates that the the norm of ggg is
bounded by ρ.
7.2  Experiments
Robust machine learning. We run ML experiments on a
machine with AMD EPYC 7542 32-Core Processor, NVIDIA
GeForce RTX 4090 and 125GB of Memory. We evaluate our
scheme on three datasets, which contain both regression and
classification tasks, and corresponding network models, as
shown in Table 2. We fix the number of data owners in the
data market N = 4.

We propose OmniLytics+, a smart-contract based decentral-
ized data market for ML applications. OmniLytics+ simul-
taneously achieves model privacy for the model owner, data
privacy for the data owners, and robustness against malicious
data owners during the trading process. We further utilize
off-chain processing to reduce the storage and computation

costs of OmniLytics+, reducing the on-chain gas cost and
expediting the trading session.
